coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of hybrid Pd@Ag core-shell and fully alloyed bi-metallic AgPd NPs and SERS enhancement of Rhodamine 6G by a unique mixture approach with graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Pandit, Sanchaya
Kunwar, Sundar
Kulkarni, Rakesh
Mandavka, Rutuja
Lin, Shusen
Lee, Jihoon
coredata.dc:description: In this work, an innovative and simple growth approach of two-step solid state dewetting (SSD) has been introduced for the fabrication of bimetallic and core-shell nanoparticle (NP) configurations. A significant surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) enhancement on the hybrid Pd@Ag core-shell NP substrate is systematically demonstrated by a unique hybrid approach of mixing the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and Rhodamine 6G (R6G). Due to the substantially increased electromagnetic hotspots in the hybrid Pd@Ag core-shell NP configuration and also the charge transfer enhancement by the addition of GQDs through the chemical enhancement mechanism, over 100 times of SERS signal enhancement is observed for the detection of R6G. The hybrid Pd@Ag core-shell NPs with the background Ag NPs and fully alloyed bi-metallic AgPd NPs without the background Ag NPs are demonstrated on sapphire (0001). The bimetallic and core-shell NPs demonstrate much enhanced localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) and thus significantly improved SERS responses. In specific, the hybrid Pd@Ag core-shell NPs demonstrate a gradual blue-shift and narrowing of LSPR peaks while the fully alloyed bi-metallic AgPd NPs show a continuing redshift and broadening of LSPR peaks.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fabrication of Pd@Ag core-shell nanoparticles (NPs) and fully alloyed bi-metallic AgPd NPs. (a) Pure Pd NPs fabricated from the sputter deposited 10 nm Pd film at 800 °C for 450 s on sapphire. (a-1) Extinction of Pd NPs. (b) Schematic of Ag coating on the Pd NP template. (c) - (c-2) Schematic, SEM image and corresponding extinction spectra of hybrid Pd@Ag core-shell NPs with the Ag NPs on the background. (d) - (d-2) Schematic SEM image and corresponding extinction spectra of fully alloyed bi-metallic AgPd NPs. (e) Schematic of surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) signal enhancement on the Pd@Ag core-shell NPs. (f) SERS enhancement with the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) added.

Pd@Ag core-shell NPs with 10 nm Ag coating followed by annealing at 400 & 600 °C on the Pd NP templates. (a) – (d) AFM top-views of 3 × 3 µm2 with the corresponding FFT patterns. (a-1) – (d-1) Magnified AFM side-views (500 × 500 nm2) of background of each sample without the primary particles. (a-2) – (d-2) Cross-sectional line-profiles on the background. (b-3) – (d-3) Magnified 3-D side-views (400 × 400 nm2) of typical primary NPs. (b-4) – (d-4) Cross-sectional line-profiles for corresponding samples. (e) Schematic representation of the annealed samples, illustrating the decrease in the height and diameter of primary NPs. (f) Plot of SAR. (g) Plots of Rq1 for the whole sample area and Rq2 for the background area. (h) – (i) Average diameter and height variation as a function of annealing temperature.

Pd@Ag core-shell NPs with 5 nm Ag coating on the Pd NP template followed by annealing at 400 & 600 °C. (a) – (d) Schematic representations at each stage. (a-1) – (d-1) AFM top-views (3 × 3 µm2) with the corresponding FFT patterns. (a-2) – (d-2) Magnified AFM top-views (1 × 1 µm2). (a-3) SAR and Rq plots of samples. (b-3) – (d-3) Cross-sectional line-profiles on background NPs (blue lines). (e) Large scale SEM images of sample annealed at 600 °C. (e-1) EDS spectra. (e-2) EDS elemental composition table of Ag and Pd. (e-3) – (e-5) Magnified SEM image and corresponding Pd, and Ag EDS elemental mappings.

Optical properties of Pd@Ag core-shell NPs with 5 nm Ag coating on the Pd NP templates. (a) – (c) Extinction (E), reflectance (R), and transmittance (T) (ERT) spectra in 270–1100 nm wavelength region. (a-1) – (c-1) Normalized and magnified ERT spectra of peak and dip regions, showing a gradual blue-shift along with the increased annealing temperature. (a-2) – (c-2) Contour plots showing the peak or dip narrowing along with the blue-shift. (d) – (e) Schematic representation of typical Pd and hybrid core-shell Pd@Ag NPs along with their local e-field distribution simulated by finite difference time domain (FDTD).

Fully alloyed bi-metallic AgPd NPs with 20 nm Ag coating on the same 10 nm Pd NP template followed by annealing at 400 & 600 °C. (a) – (d) AFM top-views of 3 × 3 µm2 with the corresponding FFT patterns. (a-1) – (d-1) Magnified 3-D AFM side-views of 500 × 500 nm2. (a-2) – (d-2) Cross-sectional line-profiles. (b-3) – (d-3) Height distribution histograms. (e) Plots of SAR and Rq. (f) – (f-3) SEM image and corresponding EDS elemental mapping of Pd and Ag with the EDS spectrum of sample annealed at 600 °C.

Optical properties of fully alloyed bi-metallic AgPd NPs with the 20 nm Ag coating on the Pd NP templates. (a) – (c) ERT spectra in 270–1100 nm region. (a-1) – (c-1) Normalized and zoomed-in spectra of peak and dip regions, showing a gradual red-shift of peaks and dips along with the increase in annealing temperature. (a-2) – (c-2) Normalized spectra normalized in both x- and y-axes, showing the broadening of peaks and dips along with the temperature increase. (a-3) Contour plot showing the shift and narrowing in the extinction peak position. (d) Magnified AFM image of typical alloy AgPd NP. (e) Corresponding local e-field distribution.

Raman spectra of (a-1) bare sapphire and (a-2) Pd@Ag core-shell NPs at 600 °C on sapphire, (a-3) 10−6 M R6G on sapphire and (a-4) 10 μL of 0.25 mg/mL GQDs on sapphire. SERS spectra of (a-5) 2:1 mixture solution of 10−6 M of Rhodamine 6G (R6G) and GQDs on sapphire and (a-6) the same 2:1 mixture solution of 10−6 M R6G and GQDs on the Pd@Ag core-shell NP substrate. The SERS signal is 100 times reduced to fit in the plot. (b) Bar diagram comparing the SERS intensity at 5 different peaks of 2:1 mixture (10−6 M R6G and GQDs) on bare sapphire and Pd@Ag core-shell NP substrate. The spectral intensity on the Pd@Ag core-shell NP substrate is scaled down by 100 times.

SERS spectra with the variation of R6G molarity (M) concentration and GQDs layer on the Pd@Ag core-shell NP substrate. (a-1) – (d-1) SERS spectra with the 10−4, 10−5, 10−6, 10−8 M of R6G. (b) Bar-diagram showing peak counts at the three different wavenumbers with the variation of R6G molarity. Inset shows a digital camera photograph of ethanol solution of respective molarity. (c) Raman spectra of GQDs layer variation on the Pd@Ag core-shell NP substrate, where a single layer corresponds to the drop-casting of 4 μL of 0.25 mg/mL GQDs. (d) Bar diagram summarizing 4 peak and band intensities.

(a) SERS spectra of different ratio (2:1, 10:1, 1:1.) of 10−6 M R6G and GQDs solution measured on the Pd@Ag core-shell NP substrate (5/10 600 °C) substrate. (b) Schematic of SERS enhancement achieved from the ground state charge transfer mechanism with the GQDs (c) SERS spectra measured on the Pd@Ag core-shell NP substrate . (c-1) – (c-4) 20 μL GQDs layer, 10 μL of 10−6 M R6G, drop-casting of 10 μL 10−6 M R6G on 20 μL GQDs/AgPd substrate, 20 μL of 2:1 mixture solution. (d) Bar diagram showing the enhancement achieved with layer and mixture approach.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of hybrid Pd@Ag core-shell and fully alloyed bi-metallic AgPd NPs and SERS enhancement of Rhodamine 6G by a unique mixture approach with graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electronic Engineering, College of Electronics and Information, Kwangwoon University, Nowon-gu, Seoul 01897, South Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Electronic Engineering, College of Electronics and Information, Kwangwoon University, Nowon-gu, Seoul 01897, South Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An innovative and simple growth approach of two-step solid state dewetting (SSD) has been introduced for the fabrication of bimetallic and core-shell nanoparticle (NP) configurations. A significant surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) enhancement on the hybrid Ag(Pd) core-shell NP substrate is systematically demonstrated by a unique hybrid approach of mixing the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and Rhodamine 6G (R6G).

In this work, an innovative and simple growth approach of two-step solid state dewetting (SSD) has been introduced for the fabrication of bimetallic and core-shell nanoparticle (NP) configurations. A significant surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) enhancement on the hybrid Pd@Ag core-shell NP substrate is systematically demonstrated by a unique hybrid approach of mixing the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and Rhodamine 6G (R6G). Due to the substantially increased electromagnetic hotspots in the hybrid Pd@Ag core-shell NP configuration and also the charge transfer enhancement by the addition of GQDs through the chemical enhancement mechanism, over 100 times of SERS signal enhancement is observed for the detection of R6G. The hybrid Pd@Ag core-shell NPs with the background Ag NPs and fully alloyed bi-metallic AgPd NPs without the background Ag NPs are demonstrated on sapphire (0001). The bimetallic and core-shell NPs demonstrate much enhanced localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) and thus significantly improved SERS responses. In specific, the hybrid Pd@Ag core-shell NPs demonstrate a gradual blue-shift and narrowing of LSPR peaks while the fully alloyed bi-metallic AgPd NPs show a continuing redshift and broadening of LSPR peaks.

ce:section.ce:para: Bimetallic and core-shell nanoparticle (NP) configurations have been the subject of intensive research interests due to their distinctive properties as compared with the conventional monometallic NPs [1–6]. Bimetallic and core-shell NPs are promising candidates for the various advanced applications in the optoelectronics [1], sensors [2], photocatalysis [3], water treatment [4], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) [5] and biomedical applications [6], just to name only a few. The added properties arising from the presence of different metallic elements and enhanced properties due to the synergetic effect of unique configurations make them exceptional. The bimetallic and core-shell nanoparticle (NP) configuration can offer the extended options for the control on the shape, structure and elemental organization of NPs so that their optical [7], catalytic [8], biorecognition [9] and electronic [10] properties can be tailored based on the requirements for their applications [11]. For instance, the Raman intensity can be considerably enhanced with the Ag core-shell structures as compared to the singular Ag NPs [5]. The metallic NPs can demonstrate the controllable localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) properties, which indicates the collective oscillation of free electrons confined on the metallic NPs when excited with the incident photons. The shape, size, interparticle spacing and elemental composition of NPs can determine the LSPR peak position and bandwidth. This indicates the geometrical parameters determine the wavelength of large electromagnetic field enhancement and thus determines the absorption and scattering of photons [12–15]. The Ag NPs generally demonstrate the strong LSPR response while the Pd NPs can find their applications in the catalysis with the superior chemical reactivity and mechanical robustness. The combination of these two metallic elements in a single NP geometry could be useful, considering the applications demanding the synergy of both.

In this study, the hybrid Pd@Ag core-shell NPs with the Ag NPs on the background and also fully alloyed bi-metallic AgPd NPs without the background NPs are successfully fabricated on sapphire (0001) by employing a two-step solid state dewetting (SSD) approach. From an identical Pd NP template fabricated in the 1st step SSD as seen in Fig. 1 (a), completely different configurations of bi-metallic AgPd NPs are resulted in the 2nd step SSD based on the Ag coating thickness control as illustrated in Fig. 1(c) and (d). The fabrication of Pd NPs of well-defined size, arrangement and uniformity are fabricated on the sapphire substrate in the first stage of SSD, i.e. the Pd NP template in Fig. 1(a). Then, the second step SSD is adapted for the fabrication of various hybrid core-shell Pd@Ag and fully alloyed bi-metallic NPs by the Ag deposition on the template and subsequent annealing as illustrated in Fig. 1(c) and (d). The development of primary Pd@Ag core-shell NPs along with the secondary background Ag NPs is demonstrated with the Ag-coatings below 10 nm on the Pd NP template in Fig. 1(c). At the same time, the 20 nm Ag coating set demonstrates the fully alloyed bi-metallic AgPd NPs without the background NPs in Fig. 1(d). The two-step SSD growth approach from the identical Pd NP templates results in the completely different LSPR evolution in terms of the peak position and band width along with the completely different NP morphologies as clearly seen in Fig. 1(c-2) and (d-2). The hybrid core-shell AgPd NPs demonstrate significantly improved LSPR and SERS responses. In addition, when the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is added, the SERS enhancement of over 100 times is clearly observed as seen in Fig. 1(f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Substrate preparation and metallic NPs fabrication
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, 430 µm-thick double-side polished c-plane sapphire (0001) wafers with ± 0.1° off-axis (iNexus Inc., South Korea) were used for the fabrication of Pd@Ag core-shell nanoparticles (NPs) and fully alloyed bi-metallic AgPd NPs as a substrate. Initially, the sapphire wafers were cut into 6 × 6 mm2 and degassed in a pulsed laser deposition (PLD) chamber under 1.0 × 10−4 torr at 600 °C for 30 min. This was done to ensure the removal of trapped particulates, oxides and water vapors from the substrate and make them ready for further fabrication process. The surface morphology of degassed sapphire was examined by an atomic force microscope (AFM) scanning in ambient as shown in Fig. S1(a), which showed the surface roughness less than 0.3 nm. The average reflectance and transmittance of sapphire were ~ 13 and ~ 85% respectively as shown in Figs. S1(b) and S1(c). Then, the metallic films were deposited using a plasma-assisted sputtering with the Pd and Ag targets of 99.999% purity. The ionization current of 3 mA and base pressure of 1 × 10−1 torr was maintained constant for all depositions and the metallic film thickness was controlled by the deposition duration variation, i.e. 20 s corresponds to 1 nm thickness. In the first step, Pd NP template was fabricated on sapphire with the 10 nm Pd film by annealing at 800 °C for 450 s. For the fabrication of hybrid core-shell NPs and fully alloyed bi-metallic AgPd NPs, i.e. the second stage growth, 5, 10 and 20 nm Ag films were deposited on the Pd NPs templates and then annealed at 400 and 600 °C for 120 s. The annealing process was performed under 1 × 10−4 torr and the target temperature was attained at 4 °C/s ramping rate, which was controlled by a computer recipe program in order to maintain the consistency. The growth at each target temperature was terminated by turning off the heating system while keeping the chamber vacuum identical until the temperature was down to an ambient.

For the preparation of GQD (graphene quantum dot) solution, 0.25 mg of GQDs powder (Sigma Aldrich, USA) was dissolved in 1 ml of absolute ethanol (Sigma Aldrich, USA) and either used as mixture (1:1, 2:1 and 10:1) with the probe molecules or as a layer on the top of NPs. The average diameter of GQDs was less than 5 nm with the extinction maximum at 485 nm. The surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) effect of various samples was probed by using the Rhodamine 6G (R6G, Sigma Aldrich, USA) and Methylene blue (MB, Sigma Aldrich, USA) analyte as Raman reporters. The ethanol solution of molecules with different molarities was drop casted (20 µL) on top of the hybrid core-shell Pd@Ag (600 °C) substrate. Raman spectra were acquired by UNIRAM II (UniNanoTech, South Korea) with an excitation of power tunable 532 nm laser and the power at the sample surface was about 9 mW. The signal was accumulated for 10 times with an expose time of 0.8 sec. A 100 × objective lens was used with the slit width of 200 μm and grating of 1200 gr/mm under dark at room temperature.

Surface morphology of the fabricated NPs was measured with an AFM (XE-70, Park Systems Corp., South Korea). The overall transition in the morphology of core-shell NPs was studied with reference to the RMS roughness (Rq) and surface area ratio (SAR). The Rq represents the average surface height and is described by the relation,

, where Hn is the profile height at each pixel. SAR represents the measure of 3D surface area (As) with respect to the geometric area (Ag) given by the relation, SAR =

× 100%. Energy-dispersive x-ray spectroscope (EDS) systems (Noran System 7, Thermo Fisher, United States) and (Ultimax, Oxford Instruments, United Kingdom) were utilized for the elemental analysis. Optical response of samples was measured with a NOST system (Nostoptiks, South Korea), equipped with an ANDOR sr-500i spectrograph, CCD detector, and combined halogen-deuterium (200 ~ 1100 nm) light sources. The optical spectra, namely reflectance and transmittance, were experimentally obtained whereas the extinction spectra were derived by using the relation: extinction (%) + reflectance (%) + transmittance (%) = 100%. The electromagnetic (EM) field of the typical NPs were simulated by using the finite-difference time domain (FDTD) software (Lumerical Solutions, Canada). The complex refractive index for Pd, Ag and sapphire were obtained from the previously published references [16,17]. For alloy compositions, the dielectric constants were calculated form the pure metals based on their atomic percentage.

Fig. 1 illustrates the overall fabrication process of hybrid Pd@Ag core-shell NPs and fully alloyed bi-metallic AgPd NPs and its SERS application. The fabrication result showed two distinct growth regimes under an identical growth condition, where the resulting nanostructures depended on the thickness of Ag coating. Here, a two-step SSD approach was adapted for the fabrication of hybrid Pd@Ag core-shell and fully alloyed bi-metallic AgPd NPs. First, the fabrication of Pd NP templates from 10 nm Pd thin film on sapphire at 800 °C was the initial step as shown in Fig. 1(a) and depicted by the schematics in Fig. S2. The annealing (800 °C) of Pd thin film below its melting point (1555 °C), resulting in the formation of isolated Pd NPs, can be described by the SSD process. The SSD of Pd films can be initiated by an atomic diffusion upon annealing and driven by the surface and interface energy minimization [18,19]. The deposited Pd films may consist of numerous voids and pinholes, which can act as the nucleation sites during the dewetting process and eventually can grow larger along with the agglomeration of Pd adatoms, resulting in the isolated and compact 3-D Pd NPs as clearly shown in Fig. 1(a) and S2. Since the temperature was sufficiently high, the Pd NPs could form an equilibrium configuration with the reduced surface energy and as a result, the flat and angular facets or planes can be found [20]. The Pd NP were with the average height and diameter of ~ 50 and 250 nm, which showed the plasmonic peaks in the UV (~315 nm) and VIS (~550 nm) as shown in Fig. 1(a-1). The extinction spectra showing the peaks in the UV and VIS region are likely due to the quadrupolar (QR) and dipolar plasmonic (DR) resonance modes of Pd NPs [21]. The resonance peaks and dips in the optical spectra in Fig. S2 are the results of specific morphology, i.e. shape, size, and configuration, of Pd NPs [22,23]. The transmittance spectra showed dips corresponding to the peaks in the extinction spectra whereas the reflectance spectra showed only minor peaks in UV and VIS regions. The flat region of reflectance spectra in VIS region may be due to the enhanced backscattering of Pd NPs [24,25].

Fig. 1(b) – (d) show the schematics of the second stage SSD, namely the Ag film coating on the Pd template, which is subjected to annealing at 400 and 600 °C. Once the Pd NPs are formed, the NP sites can possess higher chemical potential or lower surface energy [24,26]. Thus, the Ag adatoms can diffuse toward the Pd NPs and coat the Pd NPs, resulting in the Pd@Ag core-shell configuration. Also at the same time, the Ag NPs can be formed in the background as well, resulting in the formation of hybrid Pd@Ag core-shell NPs with the background Ag NPs as shown in Fig. 1(c) and (c-1). The hybrid Pd@Ag core-shell NPs were observed with the Ag deposition thickness less than 10 nm in our experiments. There should be an interfacial intermixing of Ag and Pd atoms at the Pd NP sites, but the core-shell configuration was maintained with the Ag coating less 10 nm. Meanwhile, the fully alloyed AgPd NPs were observed with the 20 nm Ag deposition as shown in Fig. 1(d) and (d-1). The resulting surface morphology was much larger and elongated configuration without any background NPs. As the amount of Ag adatoms was significantly increased, the interfacial intermixing at the Pd NP sites can be much enhanced and eventually the Pd atoms can be fully consumed and mixed with the Ag adatoms. Once the Ag-Pd atoms are fully inter-mixed, the Pd NPs cannot sustain the original configuration and the equilibrium surface morphology can be re-configured depending upon the growth condition along with the redistribution of surface energy as described in Fig. 1(d).

Along with the fabrication of the hybrid Pd@Ag core-shell NPs and fully alloyed bi-metallic AgPd NPs, the LSPR peaks show the dynamic blue or red shifts along with the band narrowing or broadening as shown in Fig. 1(c-2) and (d-2), which will be discussed in the following sections in detail. In addition, SERS application is performed on the hybrid Pd@Ag core-shell NPs for the detection of R6G and MB at various concentration. In specific, the hybrid Pd@Ag core-shell NPs fabricated with 5 nm Ag coating annealed at 600 °C is chosen for the SERS application as it demonstrated the high density of background Ag NPs along with the core-shell NPs. The high density of NPs can ensure the high density of hotspots in between the NPs and substrates for the enhancement of inherently weak Raman signal as shown by the schematic in Fig. 1(e). The metallic NPs can contribute the enhancement of Raman signal through their localized electromagnetic hotspots by the LSPR. In addition, the GQDs (graphene quantum dots) were utilized for further enhancement of SERS signal, which can be achieved through the charge transfer to the Raman probe molecules. For example, the characteristic SERS peaks of R6G were enhanced by the hybrid Pd@Ag core-shell NPs (pink plot) and then nearly two orders of enhancement was observed (blue plot) by the addition of GQD as shown in Fig. 1(f).

Fig. 2 shows the fabrication of hybrid Pd@Ag core-shell NPs by coating the Pd NP template with the 10 nm Ag films and subsequent annealing at various temperatures. Initially, the 10 nm Ag thin film deposition on sapphire showed distinct grains with the surface modulation less than 10 nm as shown in Fig. 2(a) – (a-2). The surface profile for the Ag thin film and background of the 0 °C sample, namely the 10 nm Ag deposition on the Pd template, showed a similar surface modulation of ~ 10 nm on the background. The background Ag NPs showed the height about 20 nm for the 400 °C annealed sample whereas it was increased to 35 nm for the 600 °C sample as clearly shown in Fig. 2(a-2) − (d-2). Interestingly, the surface roughness of Ag NPs was gradually increased with the annealing temperature as discussed, which resulted in the decrease in the effective height and diameter of the primary Pd@Ag NPs as illustrated by the schematic in Fig. 2(e) and 3D AFM side-views and line-profiles in Fig. 2(b-3) – (d-3) and Fig. 2(b-4) – (d-4). For example, the primary Pd@Ag NPs at 0 °C sample showed the average diameter and height of ~ 250 and ~ 45 nm and then demonstrated a decreasing trend of size along with annealing as summarized in Fig. 2(h) and (i). Meanwhile, the surface area ratio (SAR) plot of the whole sample area in Fig. 2(f) showed an overall increase from ~ 5 to 10 with the increase in the annealing temperature, indicating the growth of 3D surface area that are mostly contributed by the background Ag NP growth. Fig. 2(g) shows a plot of Rq for the background (Rq1, green) and overall structure (Rq2, red). While the background Rq was gradually increasing with the annealing as described, the overall Rq was is nearly constant likely due to the compensation between the background NP growth and primary NP reduction as discussed. The gradual development of Ag NPs in the background along with the temperature can be due to the enhanced diffusion of Ag adatoms, which is a typical characteristic of SSD [26,27].

Fig. 3 shows the fabrication of hybrid Pd@Ag core-shell nanostructures with the 5 nm Ag deposition on the Pd NP template. In this set, the thickness of Ag coating was halved as compared to the previous set and the result of which is mostly reflected on the size of secondary Ag NPs in the background as shown in Fig. 3(a-1) – (d-1). The surface morphology after Ag deposition did not show much variation from the Pd template in Fig. S2. The SAR and Rq of this sample was less than 10 and 15 nm as seen in Fig. 3(a-3). Along with the annealing at 400 and 600 °C, several noticeable changes occurred. Most noticeably, the initial Pd NP configuration was retained while the NP size was gradually decreased as seen in Fig. 3(b-2) – (d-2). Meanwhile, the background Ag NP size was gradually increased and have more than 5 nm in height at 400 °C as clearly seen in Fig. 3(b-3) – (c-3). The 5 nm Ag coating set showed a similar growth trend of background Ag NPs with the increase in annealing temperature as that of 10 nm coating, but the background Ag NPs were much smaller. Fig. 3(a-3) summarizes the plot for Rq and SAR, which shows that there is a decreasing trend in Rq and increasing trends in SAR along with annealing. The decrease in Rq can be attributed to the decrease in the effective height and diameter of primary NPs while the increase in SAR can be due to the development of secondary nanoparticles in the background. The result is the compensation and competition between background NP increase and primary NP reduction.

Fig. 3(e) – 3(e-5) show the SEM and EDS analysis of 5 nm Ag coated 600 °C annealed sample. The SEM image in Fig. 3(e) and (e-3) clearly show that the Pd@Ag core-shell (primary) NPs surrounded by the smaller background (secondary) Ag NPs and the roughness (Ag NP formation) on the primary Pd@Ag core-shell NPs. Fig. 3(e-1) shows the EDS spectra conforming the presence of Pd and Ag. Fig. 3(e-2) summarizes the weight and atomic percentage of the Ag and Pd atoms present in the sample. Elemental analyses of other 5 nm Ag coated samples are provided in Figs. S10 and S11 in the supplementary. The corresponding phase maps of Pd in Fig. 3(e-4) and Ag in Fig. 3(e-5) showed a good match with the SEM image in Fig. 3(e-3). The Pd atoms were retained in the primary NP site as clearly shown in Fig. 3(e-4). The Ag map in Fig. 3(e-5) showed the Ag count on both the primary and secondary NP sites. Higher Ag intensity was observed on the primary NP site while the background Ag NPs showed weaker intensity. This can clearly indicate the preferential Ag adatoms diffusion toward the Pd NPs due to the lower surface energy or high chemical potential at the NP site as mentioned earlier [24,26,28]. In this case, the thickness of Ag coating was relatively thinner (5 nm) so the background Ag NPs could form with the high density while the Ag atoms can partially diffuse toward the Pd NPs, forming the hybrid Pd@Ag core-shell NP configuration [28]. The surface morphology and elemental analysis of SEM, EDS spectra and mapping for the sample with 10 nm Ag coating annealed at 600 °C is shown in Fig. S7. The SEM image shows more pronounced background Ag NPs as compared with the sample by the 5 nm Ag coating. The density of secondary Ag NPs was lower than the previous set, however, the NP size were increased as the coating thickness was doubled. Annealing at the second step dewetting was done at much below Pd template fabrication temperature (800 °C) and generally Pd adatoms do not show much diffusion, or nanostructures can be quite stable at around these temperatures. Thus, the second step dewetting might not have severely affected the morphology of Pd template [29]. The stability of Pd NPs in terms of shape can be subject to the thickness of Ag coating or amount of Ag atoms [30].

Fig. 4 shows the optical properties of hybrid Pd@Ag core-shell NPs fabricated with the 5 nm Ag coating on the Pd NP templates. The optical spectra were quite sensitive to the change in the morphology and demonstrated significant variations in the intensity and position of peaks and dips at specific wavelength. The Pd NP template demonstrated the DR and QR at 525 and 315 nm respectively. In the case of pure 5 nm Ag film on sapphire, it had much stronger peaks at 560 and 390 nm. The nanoscale Ag thin film showed much stronger VIS peak intensity as compared to the Pd NP template likely due to the stronger plasmonic response of Ag than Pd and presence of nanoscale grains [31,32]. The hybrid Pd@Ag core-shell NPs showed an intermediate optical behavior between pure Pd NPs and pure smaller Ag NPs (~400 nm) likely due to the inherited properties of both. In addition, the extinction spectra for the Ag-coated Pd templates showed an increase in the resonance peak intensity as compared to the individual Ag coating and Pd template as in Fig. 4(a). Fig. 4(a-1) – (a-2) show the normalized extinction peaks and corresponding contour plots to visualize the trend of LSPR peaks. The hybrid Pd@Ag core-shell NPs showed a gradual blue-shift in the resonance peak toward the shorter wavelength with the increase in annealing temperature as clearly shown in Fig. 4(a-2). Specifically, the extinction peak for the 0 °C sample has a resonance peak at ~ 520 nm, the 400 °C sample showed a peak at ~ 490 nm and the 600 °C sample showed a peak at ~ 480 nm. Also, the narrowing of resonance peaks was observed with the increase in the annealing temperature. This blue shift along with the narrowing of the spectra can be attributed to a decrease in the height and diameter of primary Pd NPs [33] and the uniform formation of background Ag NPs [34,35].

In addition, the reflectance spectra are shown in Fig. 4(b) – (b-2) with the magnified view of peaks, normalization and contour plot. The reflectance peak for the Ag thin film was at 575 nm. Meanwhile, the reflectance peaks for the 0 °C, 400 °C, 600 °C samples were at 590 nm, 525 nm and 500 nm respectively. The reflectance shoulder peaks also showed the blue shift and narrowing of spectral width for the Pd@Ag core-shell NPs with the annealing as shown in Fig. 4(b-1) and (b-2). The transmittance spectra along with normalization and magnification are shown in Fig. 4(c) – (c-2). Generally, the transmittance dips were mostly at the extinction peak positions in VIS region as expected. Specifically, the dip position was at 540 nm, 500 nm and 490 nm respectively for the 0 °C, 400 °C and 600 °C samples as shown in the magnified normalized transmittance spectrum in Fig. 4(c-1). The transmittance spectra for the annealed samples also demonstrated a blue shift of dipolar dips and narrowing of spectral width as shown in Fig. 4(c-2). Similarly, the optical responses for the 10 nm Ag coating sample for hybrid Pd@Ag NPs are shown in supplementary Fig. S8. The spectra show similar behavior, blueshift and narrowing of spectral peaks with annealing. The shift for extinction spectra was about 70 nm and the spectral bandwidth were broader compared to 5 nm coating set, which may be due to aggressive growth of background NPs. Furthermore, the localized e-field of the typical template Pd NP and the hybrid Pd@Ag NP was simulated by FDTD as shown in Fig. 4(d) and (e) respectively. Along with the formation of additional hotspots between background NPs and primary NPs, a significant enhancement in the e-field intensity was observed with the hybrid Pd@Ag NPs in comparison to the pure Pd NPs, which is in correlation with the experimental results. Although the area of hotspot was decreased with the Pd@Ag NPs, the e-field intensity was increased by over 2.7 times as clearly seen in the color scale bars. Similarly, the e-field distribution of AgPd core-shell NP and Ag NP were displayed along with the FDTD simulation setup in Fig. S3.

Fig. 5 shows the surface morphology of fully alloyed bi-metallic AgPd NPs by the 20 nm Ag coating along with the evolution of NPs with temperature. The evolution of surface morphology of NPs for this set showed a drastic contrast as compared with the previous sets. First, this set showed no background Ag NPs as clearly shown in Fig. 5(c) – (d) after annealing. In more detail, the 20 nm Ag thin film showed a similar surface roughness to that of 10 nm film in the previous set but there was a formation of more pronounced grains as shown in Fig. 5(a) – 5(a-2). The 0 °C sample showed a decrease in the diameter of NPs compared to the original Pd template likely be due to the sinking of NPs with the much thicker 20 nm Ag coating. The sample after annealing at 400 °C showed the evolution of inter-connected nanoclusters as in Fig. 5(c) and the 600 °C showed the irregular NP configuration with more separation of NPs due to the enhanced surface diffusion in Fig. 5(d). The diameter of NPs was increased about twice with the annealing as seen from the line-profiles in Fig. 5(b-2) – (d-2). In addition, the height distribution histogram in Fig. 5(b-3) – (d-3) showed the average height was increased from ~ 35 nm to ~ 90 nm when the annealing temperature was increased from 0 to 600 °C. The Rq and SAR plots showed an increase from 14 nm to 38 nm and 7.35 to 27.15 respectively with the much-increased size of NPs for the alloy samples as shown in Fig. 5(e). Fig. 5(f) – 5(f-3) show the surface morphology and elemental analysis of the 20 nm Ag coated sample at 600 °C with the SEM, EDS spectra and phase maps of elements. The increased amount of Ag coating is verified with the pronounced Ag peaks in the EDS spectra in Fig. 5(f-3) and elemental composition table in Fig. S14. The phase maps of Pd and Ag in Fig. 5(f-1) and 5(f-2) showed a compete match to the SEM image with no background Ag NP formation, indicating the Ag and Pd atoms were fully mixed and formed AgPd bi-metallic alloy NPs. Indeed, slight count of Pd was observed on the background in Fig. 5(f-1) and this could be due to the minor residual Pd atoms during the diffusion and restricting of surface morphology, which was not observed by the AFM scan. In this set, the thickness of Ag coating was much increased as compared to the previous sets and was much higher than the thickness of Pd film (10 nm) used for first step dewetting. Thus, the intermixing at the interface of the Pd NP can be much enhanced with much increased available Ag adatoms. Once all the Pd atoms are consumed by the intermixing, the fully alloyed atoms can reconstruct the surface configuration with the redistribution of surface energy, thus forming the elongated AgPd alloy NPs [36,37].

Fig. 6 shows the optical properties of the fully alloyed bi-metallic AgPd NPs with the 20 nm Ag coating on the Pd template at different annealing temperature. The optical properties of fully alloyed bi-metallic AgPd NPs showed completely different behaviors as compared to the previous sets. This alteration in the optical behavior should be the result of completely different evolution trends of the NPs as discussed above. For example, the primary NPs in this set are fully alloyed bi-metallic NPs rather than the core-shell configuration. Also, there was no secondary background Ag NPs observed with the 20 nm coating. To begin with, the extinction spectra of pure 20 nm Ag thin film showed a broader peak likely due to the loss of LSPR response with the increase in the film thickness in Fig. 6(a). With the formation of fully alloyed bi-metallic AgPd NPs, first the overall extinction peak % was much increased, i.e. ~ 50% and the NIR portion of spectra was also much pronounced as compared to the previous sets, which can be due to much larger size of alloy NP after the evolution. In more detail, the 0 °C sample showed a minor DR extinction peak, which might be due to the thick Ag coating again, hindering the LSPR response. After annealing, the extinction peaks for the 400 and 600 °C samples became more dominant. As the size of bi-metallic AgPd was increased with the annealing temperature, a red shift of the extinction peaks resulted [33–35]. Specifically, the extinction peaks were at 445, 460 and 470 nm for the 0, 400 and 600 °C samples as shown in Fig. 6(a-1) and (a-3). Also, the gradual broadening of spectra at the resonance peak was observed with the increase in annealing temperature as shown in Fig. 6(a-2) – (a-3), which is a sharp contrast with the previous sets. This may be due to the gradual size increase of NPs along with annealing, resulting in the red-shift [33–35]. The resonance peaks take place when the frequency of incoming light wave approaches the eigenfrequency of NPs, which is proportional to the radius [33]. The reflectance spectra of the bi-metallic alloy AgPd NPs in Fig. 6(b) demonstrated the gradual decrease along with annealing without any overlapping of spectrum and there was a clear reflectance dips in the extinction peak positions. The red-shift and broadening of spectra at peak position were also valid for the reflectance spectra as shown in Fig. 6(b-1) and (b-2) respectively. The transmittance spectra of bi-metallic alloy AgPd NPs at different annealing condition are shown in Fig. 6(c) – (c-2). There was a shoulder formation in the transmittance spectra at the corresponding peak position of extinction spectra, which may be due to forward scattering of larger bi-metallic AgPd NPs [25]. The red shift of peaks and the broadening of spectra at the peak position also held true for the transmittance, which may be due to the increase of size of AgPd NPs as discussed for other spectra. Finally, the e-field distribution of typical AgPd alloy NP fabricated at 600 ⁰C was simulated as shown in Fig. 6(d) and (e), which exhibited a strong e-field confinement at the sharp edges of the NPs. The e-field intensity of AgPd alloy NP was also much stronger as compared to the pure Pd NP as presented in the previous set.

Fig. 7 shows various Raman and SERS signals measured on the bare sapphire (0001) and hybrid Pd@Ag core-shell NP substrate by the 5 nm Ag deposition and subsequent annealing at 600 °C. All the SERS measurement of probe molecules were performed on the hybrid Pd@Ag core-shell NPs. This hybrid NP substrate has the high density of background Ag NPs with the small interparticle spacing and thus can provide greater number of hotspots for the enhancement of Raman signals. To begin with, one of the characteristic Raman peaks of bare sapphire (0001) is shown at 750 cm−1 in Fig. 7(a-1). Indeed, the sapphire (0001) possess the Raman peaks at 418 cm−1 due to the A1g and at 379, 447, 578, and 750 cm−1 due to the Eg vibration modes [38]. As known, the hybrid Pd@Ag core-shell NP itself shows no Raman signal in Fig. 7(a-2) and the application of 10−6 M of Rhodamine 6G (R6G) (10 μL) on the bare sapphire also shows no characteristic peaks in Fig. 7(a-3). The sapphire is insensitive to Raman enhancement and indeed, the Eg vibration mode of sapphire at 750 cm−1 is slightly reduced with the application of R6G. With the application of GQDs (graphene quantum dots) on the bare sapphire, the amorphous D and graphitic G bands were observed at 1350 and 1585 cm−1 respectively in accordance with other studies [39]. Interestingly, when the R6G was mixed with the GQDs (2:1 mixture ratio in this case), the characteristic R6G peaks were directly visible on sapphire as shown in Fig. 7(a-5), indicating the GQDs can contribute as the SERS agent. For example, the 612-peak showed 700 counts and 770-peak showed 375 count as well summarized in Fig. 7(b) and Table S1. Even more interestingly, the SERS signal was significantly increased by a factor of 100 when the same solution was applied on the hybrid Pd@Ag core-shell NP substrate in Fig. 7(a-6) and (b). As clearly seen in Fig. 7(b), the SERS intensity comparison for the 5 characteristic peaks of R6G is presented on the sapphire and NP substrate and the hybrid Pd@Ag core-shell NP substrate demonstrated more than 2 orders of enhancement for the 2:1 mixture of R6G and GQDs as compared to the bare sapphire.

Fig. 8 (a) shows the concentration dependent SERS of R6G for the 10−4 M to 10−8 M on the hybrid Pd@Ag core-shell NP substrate. In this case, the probe molecule binding should be only with Ag atoms as the Pd primary NPs were fully encapsulated by the Ag. Thus, the binding of probe molecule should be uniform throughout the surface of NPs samples. The SERS signal was gradually increased with the increased molarity as clearly summarized in Fig. 8(a) and (b) as expected and the characteristic peaks of R6G at 612, 775, 1187, 1309, 1360, 1506, 1569 and 1648 cm−1 are in agreement with the other reported works [40]. The enhancement of Raman signal obtained with the hybrid core-shell NP substrate can be mostly attributed to the electromagnetic mechanism [41]. The localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) of hybrid NPs can demonstrate the electromagnetic fields on the NPs, which can create the plasmonic hotspots on the sharp edges and also where there are small gaps. These plasmonic hotspots can amplify both the excitation and emission radiation and thus can enhance the weak Raman signals, achieving the SERS [41]. The probe molecules adsorbed on the core-shell NPs near the hotspots experiences large electric fields, resulting in the enhanced Raman intensity. Fig. 8(c) shows the Raman spectra of GQDs (graphene quantum dots) applied on the same hybrid NP substrate with the gradual addition of 4 μL of 0.25 mgmL−1 at each layer. The enhancement of D and G peaks along with the other new smaller peaks was clearly observed along with the increased number of GQDs layers, improving the Raman scattering. The new smaller peaks other than the D and G vibrational modes were observed with the GQDs layer on the hybrid NP substrate. Although it was not reported before, it might be caused by the adsorption of topological edge defect of GQDs [42,43]. The Raman intensity is summarized as a function of GQDs layers in Fig. 8(d), which clearly shows a gradual increase along with the addition of GQDs layer. In addition, the methylene blue (MB) was used as a probe molecule on the hybrid core-shell NP substrate and the SERS up to 10−3 M showed a similar trend of gradually increased intensity in Figs. S15(a) and S15(b). The GQDs on the bare sapphire showed the gradual enhancement of D and G peaks with the addition in Figs. S15(c) and S15(d).

Fig. 9 shows the SERS results to further study the enhancement achieved with the mixture solution on the hybrid Pd@Ag core-shell NP substrate. Fig. 9(a) shows the corresponding SERS spectra from the mixture of 10−6 M R6G and GQDs of various ratios as labeled. All the mixture solutions showed the enhanced SERS of R6G as compared to the pure 10−6 M R6G in Fig. 9(a). Specifically, the 2:1 mixture solution showed the greatest enhancement while the 10:1 and 1:1 ratio showed slightly less enhancement. The interparticle spacing of background Ag NPs in the hybrid configuration can be reduced with the application of GQDs. The high-density small gaps achieved with the NPs and GQDs can act as the electromagnetic hotspots, which can enhance the Raman emission from the electromagnetic perspective. In addition, the chemical enhancement mechanism (CM) can be achieved from the contact degree between the Pd@Ag NPs and R6G with the help of GQD [44]. This could be ascribed to the more accessible edges and dangling bonds of GQDs. At the same time, the GQDs have the π – π layer structure in nature and a closer contact with the R6G molecules can be possible through the π - π stacking [45]. Thus, the GQDs can act as an interfacial layer between the plasmonic NPs and probe molecules, which could facilitate an efficient charge transfer to the probe molecules. Furthermore, the hydrophilicity of GQDs can also enhance the adsorption of R6G through the electrostatic attraction [46]. The CM is a short-range effect occurring at the molecular scale and the close interaction between GQDs and R6G can help the charge transfer from the GQDs to the HOMO level of R6G at the ground state as described in schematic in Fig. 9(b) [47]. The charge transfer from the LUMO to the conduction band (CB) of GQDs and from the valance band (VB) to the HOMO of R6G is also possible with the laser excitation [48]. The hybrid configuration of Pd@Ag NP and GQDs substrate with the large NPs can provide enough surface area for the adsorption of R6G molecules along with the capture of the incident light for the significantly enhanced SERS. The 2:1 mixture solution might be providing more favorable interparticle spacings and thus can contribute to the highest SERS enhancement with the appropriate amount of GQDs. Meanwhile, the 10:1 mixture showed slightly less enhancement in Fig. 9(a-2), which might be due to insufficient amount of GQDs contributing to the enhancement. In contrast, the 1:1 mixture solution showed further reduced enhancement, which can be due to the saturation of hotspot with the increased amount of GQDs and blocking of R6G Raman signals. In addition, the high amount of GQDs could likely block the gaps between NPs, resulting in the reduced e-field intensity. Fig. 9(c) summarizes the enhancement achieved with the different methods used with the 10−6 M R6G on the hybrid Pd@Ag core-shell NP substrate. The 20 μL GQDs layer showed the D and G peaks with other new smaller peaks in Fig. 9(c-1) and the 10−6 M of R6G clearly shows all the characteristics peaks of R6G on the hybrid NP substrate in Fig. 9(c-2). The GQDs peaks were buried when the R6G is drop casted on the GQD layer / hybrid NPs substrate. In this case, the GQDs layer was made first and then the10−6 M of R6G was applied, i.e. not a mixture but layer and R6G application. The three characteristic peaks at 612, 1360, and 1648 cm−1 is compared in Fig. 9(d) for the pure 10−6 M R6G, GQDs layer and 2:1 mixture solution. The corresponding peak intensity at 612 cm−1 is enhanced about 6 and 20 times with GQDs layer and 2:1 mixture solution, respectively. Meanwhile, the peaks at 1360 and 1648 cm−1 are enhanced about 4 and 11 times with the layer and 2:1 mixture solution, respectively. The 2:1 mixture application on the hybrid NP substrate demonstrated the best performance with the large number of accessible edges of GQDs [44]. On the edge of GQDs, dangling bonds exist, which probably act as hotspots to effectively adsorb or trap more probe molecules. Similarly, the application of GQDs mixed with the MB for different molarity is shown in Fig. S16 and the SERS with / without the GQDs is summarized in Fig. S17. In short, the high GQDs concentration, i. e. 1:1 and 2:1, showed more enhancement with the high molarity while the lower GQDs concentration showed better enhancement with the lower molarity. Generally, the GQDs application can be more useful for the lower molarity detection as summarized in Fig. S17.

In summary, two distinctive morphologies of plasmonic NPs, i. e. the hybrid Pd@Ag core-shell NPs with the Ag NPs on the background and fully alloyed bi-metallic AgPd NPs without the background Ag NPs, were demonstrated on sapphire (0001). Based on the thickness control of Ag coating utilized in the second step SSD, the Ag-Pd NPs showed the completely different growth trends. They showed the opposite LSPR peak shifting trend. The hybrid Pd@Ag core-shell NPs showed a gradual blue shift and narrowing of LSPR peaks as a function of annealing temperature whereas the fully alloyed bi-metallic AgPd NPs showed a gradual red shift and broadening of LSPR peaks. In addition, a unique approach to enhance the SERS was adapted by mixing the probe molecules with the GQDs (graphene quantum dots), which demonstrated more than two order increment of SERS intensity. Depending on the probe molecules and mixture solution ratio, different degree of enhancement was demonstrated. The variation was explained with the interparticle spacing and hotspot saturation.
